text:
GASTROINTESTINAL MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
POST-OPERATIVE FOLLOW-UP NOTE — 6-WEEK VISIT AFTER PANCREATICODUODENECTOMY

---

ATTENDING: Gastrointestinal Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Return Office Visit
REASON FOR VISIT: Post-operative surveillance and adjuvant therapy planning following pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African woman with a history of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the head and uncinate process who presents for her 6-week follow-up after pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (PPPD). She was discharged from the hospital approximately 4 weeks ago following a prolonged admission for a post-operative grade B pancreatic fistula. She is here to discuss pathology results in full, ongoing recovery, management of new post-surgical complications, and planning for adjuvant systemic therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu was initially diagnosed with borderline resectable PDAC of the pancreatic head and uncinate process (cT4 N1 M0, Stage III, AJCC 8th edition) approximately nine months ago. Her presenting CA 19-9 was markedly elevated at 1,247 U/mL at diagnosis, and her CEA was 8.4 ng/mL. Staging workup including multiphasic CT with pancreatic protocol and FDG PET-CT confirmed a 3.8 x 3.2 cm hypoenhancing mass with SMA contact exceeding 180 degrees of circumference and partial SMV occlusion. Histologic diagnosis was established by EUS-guided fine needle biopsy demonstrating moderately differentiated infiltrating ductal carcinoma with SMAD4 loss. Comprehensive molecular profiling revealed KRAS G12V and TP53 R248W mutations, SMAD4 and CDKN2A homozygous deletions, and a germline ATM pathogenic frameshift variant (p.V2716fs, confirmed on germline testing). MSS/MSI-stable, TMB-low, PD-L1 CPS 2.

She was taken to multidisciplinary tumor board which recommended neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) given her ECOG 1 performance status and absence of distant metastatic disease. She completed 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX (oxali 85 mg/m2 reduced to 68 mg/m2 after cycle 2 for grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy; iri 150 mg/m2 per dose-reduced starting regimen; leucovorin 400 mg/m2; 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 over 46-hour infusion without bolus). She had meaningful CA 19-9 decline to 85 U/mL and radiographic partial response with tumor reduction from 3.8 cm to 2.6 cm and SMA contact decreasing to approximately 90 degrees. She then underwent consolidative conventionally fractionated chemoradiation — 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent cape 825 mg/m2 twice daily on radiation days — which further reduced the primary lesion to 2.3 x 1.9 cm with SMA contact estimated at 60-70 degrees and an essentially patent SMV. CA 19-9 was 72 U/mL at that point, declining further to 48 U/mL by the time of surgery.

She underwent pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy approximately 6 weeks ago. Intraoperative findings were favorable: the primary tumor was in the pancreatic head/uncinate, grossly adherent to but separable from the SMA; the SMV was dissected free without venous reconstruction; no peritoneal implants or hepatic metastases were encountered. Intraoperative frozen sections of the pancreatic neck margin and bile duct margin were negative. Reconstruction was via duct-to-mucosa pancreaticojejunostomy, hepaticojejunostomy, and duodenojejunostomy. Estimated blood loss was 650 mL; one unit of packed red blood cells was transfused. A Jackson-Pratt drain was placed near the pancreaticojejunostomy.

Her post-operative course was complicated by a clinically relevant grade B pancreatic fistula (per ISGPF criteria): drain amylase peaked at approximately 4,800 U/L on post-operative day 7, drain output was 180 mL/day, and she had low-grade fever to 38.1°C without systemic sepsis. She was managed conservatively with bowel rest, total parenteral nutrition, and octreotide 100 mcg subcutaneous TID. Her hospital stay was extended to 18 days. She was discharged home with the drain in situ and returned to the clinic approximately 3 weeks ago where the drain was removed once output had decreased to minimal levels and amylase had normalized — consistent with fistula resolution at approximately 5 weeks post-operatively.

Today at 6 weeks post-operatively she reports gradual improvement in energy levels, though she remains fatigued. She is tolerating a soft-to-regular diet but notes she has experienced loose, greasy stools with a foul odor since the drain was removed, consistent with steatorrhea. She reports approximately 3.5 kg of weight loss from her pre-operative weight and has had difficulty maintaining caloric intake due to early satiety and intermittent nausea. She has been checking her blood glucose at home and reporting significant hyperglycemia since the surgery, which the endocrinology team has already begun addressing. She has mild bilateral distal extremity paresthesias in the fingertips and toes, which she reports as slightly better than during chemotherapy. She denies abdominal pain, jaundice, acholic stools (separate from the steatorrhea), hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia. No fevers or chills since discharge. She is managing her surgical incision without difficulty.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Fatigue (moderate), weight loss of approximately 3.5 kg from pre-surgical baseline, decreased appetite with early satiety.
Gastrointestinal: Steatorrhea (loose, greasy, malodorous stools), intermittent nausea (mild), early satiety. No abdominal pain. No frank diarrhea. No jaundice. No nausea/vomiting requiring medication.
Musculoskeletal/Neurologic: Bilateral distal sensory paresthesias of fingertips and toes (grade 1, residual oxali neuropathy, stable/slowly improving). No new focal neurologic symptoms.
Endocrine/Metabolic: Newly insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus post-Whipple; blood glucose variability at home (fingerstick readings 130-280 mg/dL).
Integumentary: Mild plantar tenderness from prior hand-foot syndrome (now grade 1 or resolved).
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.
Pulmonary: No dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria.
Psychosocial: Reports significant anxiety regarding pathology results and adjuvant therapy decisions; family is present and supportive.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, head/uncinate process — treated as above (neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX x 8 cycles, consolidative chemoradiation 50.4 Gy/cape, PPPD — see HPI)
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus, 10+ years (previously on metformin, now new-onset insulin-dependent post-Whipple)
3. Hypertension
4. Post-operative grade B pancreatic fistula (resolved)
5. Germline ATM heterozygous pathogenic variant (p.V2716fs) — identified during oncologic workup; genetic counseling completed
6. Mild residual peripheral sensory neuropathy — Eloxatin-related, grade 1, improving

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old retired schoolteacher of West African descent. She lives alone but has strong family support; several family members accompanied her to today's visit. She is a lifelong nonsmoker and denies significant alcohol use. She is active in her community and church. BMI today approximately 22.8 (mildly reduced from pre-diagnosis BMI of 24.1). She is fully ambulatory and independent with activities of daily living.

FAMILY HISTORY:
No family history of pancreatic cancer. No known first-degree relatives with germline ATM-related malignancies (breast, ovarian, prostate). Germline ATM finding has been disclosed; cascade testing for family members discussed.

ALLERGIES:
No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS (Current):
1. Insulin glargine (basal) — dose per endocrinology; initiated post-operatively for new-onset insulin-dependent diabetes
2. Insulin aspart (rapid-acting, with meals) — dose per endocrinology
3. Metformin — HELD post-operatively given new insulin dependence and post-Whipple physiology; under endocrinology review
4. Amlodipine 5 mg daily (hypertension)
5. Lisinopril 10 mg daily (hypertension)
6. Omeprazole 40 mg twice daily (ongoing, for radiation-induced gastritis prophylaxis and post-Whipple gastric protection)
7. Ondansetron 8 mg PRN (nausea)
8. Loperamide PRN (loose stools; not actively requiring)
9. Multivitamin with vitamin D and B12 supplementation (post-gastrointestinal surgery protocol)
10. Pancrelipase (PERT) — newly initiated today; see plan
Prior medications completed/discontinued: Pegfilgrastim (neoadjuvant, discontinued), Xeloda (chemoradiation course completed), octreotide (post-fistula management, completed)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 138/84 mmHg, Heart rate 74 bpm (regular), Temperature 36.8°C, Respiratory rate 16, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight: 57.8 kg (pre-operative weight approximately 61.3 kg; loss of 3.5 kg).
General: Alert and oriented, mildly fatigued in appearance but in no acute distress. Cooperative and engaged in discussion.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Anicteric sclerae. No lymphadenopathy in cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary chains.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheezes, or pleural rub.
Abdomen: Well-healing midline surgical incision (Kocher/midline variant) without erythema, dehiscence, or signs of wound infection. Abdomen is soft. There is mild tenderness to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant and epigastrium at the surgical bed, appropriate for 6-week post-operative state. No guarding or rigidity. No palpable mass. Bowel sounds present. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly on palpation. No ascites appreciated on percussion.
Extremities: No cyanosis or clubbing. Mild bilateral distal plantar callusing consistent with prior hand-foot syndrome, no active breakdown. Peripheral sensation decreased to light touch in bilateral distal fingers and toes (consistent with grade 1 oxali neuropathy).
Neurologic: Cranial nerves grossly intact. Strength 5/5 throughout. Gait within normal limits.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
CA 19-9: 48 U/mL (post-operative; notable for dramatic decline from pre-treatment 1,247 U/mL; within or approaching normal range, threshold <37 U/mL)
CEA: 3.1 ng/mL (improved from 8.4 ng/mL at diagnosis)
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 138, Potassium 3.9, Chloride 102, Bicarbonate 24, BUN 14, Creatinine 0.78 mg/dL (eGFR >60), ALT 28 U/L, AST 32 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 72 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, Albumin 3.3 g/dL (mildly low, consistent with surgical/nutritional recovery)
Complete blood count: WBC 6.8 x10^9/L, Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL (mild anemia, recovering from perioperative blood loss and chemotherapy), Platelets 218 x10^9/L, MCV 88 fL
HbA1c: 8.6% (elevated; consistent with perioperative hyperglycemia and new post-pancreatectomy endocrine insufficiency; under active endocrinology management)
Fasting glucose today: 187 mg/dL
Fecal elastase-1 (collected at home prior to visit): 148 mcg/g (below 200 mcg/g threshold; confirms exocrine pancreatic insufficiency)
Vitamin B12: 342 pg/mL (low-normal; B12 supplementation ongoing)
Vitamin D (25-OH): 22 ng/mL (mildly deficient; supplementation to be increased)
Prothrombin time/INR: 1.1 (normal)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
No new imaging obtained at this visit. Most recent CT abdomen/pelvis was performed at approximately 8 months from initial diagnosis (2 months after chemoradiation completion, immediately prior to surgery), which showed the primary lesion at 2.3 x 1.9 cm, SMA contact at 60-70 degrees, patent SMV, and no hepatic lesions or peritoneal disease. Post-operative surveillance imaging will be planned for approximately 3 months post-operatively as per our standard pancreatic cancer follow-up protocol.

---

PATHOLOGY REVIEW (Surgical Specimen — Final Report Reviewed Today in Detail with Patient):
The final Whipple specimen pathology has been reviewed in its entirety with Ms. Osei-Bonsu and her family. Key findings discussed:
- Histology: Moderately differentiated (grade 2) pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, pancreatic head and uncinate process
- Tumor size: 2.1 x 1.8 cm (significantly reduced from the pre-treatment 3.8 cm primary, reflecting treatment response)
- Treatment effect: CAP tumor regression grade 2 — moderate response; residual viable carcinoma present but with evident areas of fibrosis and necrosis attributable to neoadjuvant therapy. This is an intermediate response category and is consistent with her radiographic and serologic response.
- Surgical margins: All margins are NEGATIVE (R0 resection). This is the critical finding I discussed with patient at length. The pancreatic neck margin has 0.4 cm clearance, the posterior retroperitoneal/SMA margin is negative at 0.1 cm (close but genuinely negative), and the uncinate, bile duct, anterior, and distal margins are all negative.
- Lymph nodes: 0 out of 22 lymph nodes examined show metastatic involvement (ypN0). This is an outstanding result — all 22 sampled regional nodes are free of cancer.
- Pathologic stage after neoadjuvant therapy: ypT2 ypN0 cM0, Stage IIA (AJCC 8th edition). Downstaged from clinical T4 N1 to pathologic T2 N0.
- Lymphovascular invasion: focally present. Perineural invasion: present. These are noted but do not change management at this time.
- Background pathology: Chronic pancreatitis, pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PanIN) grades 1-2 in the non-neoplastic pancreatic parenchyma.

I emphasized to the patient that achieving an R0 resection with complete lymph node clearance after neoadjuvant therapy is the best possible surgical outcome for this diagnosis, and represents a meaningful accomplishment given the extent of disease at presentation. The significance of the close SMA margin was discussed; while negative at 0.1 cm, this margin was addressed by neoadjuvant therapy and chemoradiation, and no further local therapy is indicated at this time. The presence of residual perineural invasion and lymphovascular invasion on pathology underscores the rationale for proceeding with adjuvant systemic therapy.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. POST-OPERATIVE RECOVERY FOLLOWING R0 WHIPPLE PROCEDURE (ypT2N0M0 PANCREATIC ADENOCARCINOMA):
Ms. Osei-Bonsu has achieved an excellent surgical result following a prolonged and intensive multimodality neoadjuvant treatment course. She is now approximately 6 weeks post-operatively, recovering appropriately, with resolution of her grade B pancreatic fistula. Her overall trajectory is encouraging: CA 19-9 of 48 U/mL at this visit — down from a pre-treatment peak of 1,247 U/mL — reflects the cumulative tumor burden reduction through neoadjuvant and surgical phases of treatment. I expect further normalization on subsequent checks once she is on adjuvant therapy and the post-operative period progresses. We will proceed with adjuvant therapy planning as outlined below.

2. PANCREATIC EXOCRINE INSUFFICIENCY — INITIATION OF PERT:
Fecal elastase-1 is 148 mcg/g, confirming pancreatic exocrine insufficiency (EPI), a near-universal consequence of pancreaticoduodenectomy given resection of the pancreatic head. This explains her steatorrhea and likely contributes to her weight loss and nutritional deficit. I am initiating pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy today:
- Pancrelipase (Creon) 40,000 lipase units orally with each main meal and 20,000 lipase units with snacks. Advise patient to take with the first bite of food, not before or after. Enzymes must not be crushed.
- Dietary counseling: She should follow a low-fat diet transiently while enzymes are titrated, with gradual liberalization. Referral to our oncology dietitian placed today.
- Supplemental oral nutrition (Ensure Plus or equivalent) to continue as a caloric supplement between meals.
- Anticipate progressive improvement in stool consistency and weight stabilization within 3-4 weeks of PERT initiation.
- Vitamin supplementation: Increase vitamin D3 to 2,000 IU daily; continue B12 supplementation. Monitor fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) at next visit.

3. NEW-ONSET POST-PANCREATECTOMY INSULIN-DEPENDENT DIABETES MELLITUS (TYPE 3c):
Resection of the pancreatic head and uncinate process has caused what is effectively type 3c (pancreatogenic) diabetes, superimposed on her prior type 2 diabetes. Post-Whipple diabetes can be particularly brittle due to loss of both insulin-secreting beta cells and glucagon-secreting alpha cells, creating risk for hypoglycemia as well as hyperglycemia. Her endocrinology team is actively managing her insulin regimen. Today's HbA1c is 8.6% and fasting glucose is 187 mg/dL, indicating suboptimal control that needs to be addressed prior to chemotherapy initiation, as hyperglycemia worsens chemotherapy tolerability and infection risk. I have communicated directly with her endocrinologist. Metformin is being held, appropriately. Goals: FBG 100-140, post-prandial <200 prior to adjuvant therapy start. Continue close follow-up with endocrinology.

4. ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC CHEMOTHERAPY — PLANNING:
Given her pathologic staging (ypT2N0M0) and the presence of residual viable carcinoma with lymphovascular and perineural invasion, adjuvant systemic therapy is strongly indicated. The standard of care for adjuvant PDAC following resection — particularly after neoadjuvant therapy — is an area of evolving evidence. Given that she received neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX (oxali-containing), I discussed the following options:

a) gem plus cape (ESPAC-4 regimen): This is a well-validated adjuvant regimen with proven overall survival benefit over gem monotherapy. Given she has not received a gem-based backbone previously, her tumor would have a relatively chemotherapy-naive response to this combination. This is my preferred recommendation.

b) Gemzar monotherapy: A historical standard with less toxicity; considered in patients with poor performance status or significant comorbidities. Given her ECOG 1 status, I favor the combination.

c) Further mFOLFIRINOX/FOLFOX-based adjuvant therapy: Less favored given prior exposure, cumulative neuropathy burden, and limited data supporting this approach following neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX.

Recommendation: Proceed with adjuvant Gemzar 1000 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, and 15 of a 28-day cycle, plus Xeloda 1660 mg/m2/day orally in two divided doses on days 1-21, for 6 cycles (approximately 6 months). Treatment intent is adjuvant with curative intent. Target start approximately 8-10 weeks post-operatively (2-4 weeks from today) pending adequate recovery and glucose control. Pre-authorization submitted.

Pre-chemotherapy labs to be obtained in 2 weeks: CBC with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, CA 19-9. Repeat HbA1c. ECOG reassessment at that visit.

Cumulative Eloxatin neuropathy: Grade 1 currently (stable, slowly improving). The planned adjuvant regimen does not include Eloxatin, which is appropriate given her prior exposure. Xeloda re-exposure is planned; prior hand-foot syndrome was grade 1 during chemoradiation at 825 mg/m2 twice daily — the full adjuvant dose is higher and she should be counseled to monitor for HFS, use urea-based emollients on palms and soles, and report early symptoms.

5. GERMLINE ATM PATHOGENIC VARIANT:
Germline ATM heterozygous pathogenic variant (p.V2716fs) confirmed on testing. Patient has been counseled on the implications for her own cancer risk (increased risk for other malignancy subtypes including possibly breast) and the autosomal dominant inheritance pattern (50% chance of transmission to each first-degree relative). She has been referred to genetic counseling for ongoing management and family cascade testing. No immediate change to cancer management is indicated by this finding in the adjuvant/post-resection setting. PARP inhibitor use is generally not approved in ATM-mutated PDAC outside clinical trial context at this time.

6. SURVEILLANCE PLANNING:
Post-resection surveillance schedule:
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast every 3 months for the first 2 years post-surgery
- CA 19-9 and CEA with each imaging cycle
- First post-operative surveillance CT planned approximately 3 months from surgery (1-2 months from today)
- Oncology clinic follow-up every 3 months

7. COMORBIDITY MANAGEMENT:
- Hypertension: Blood pressure today 138/84 mmHg; current antihypertensive regimen continued; follow-up with PCP.
- Nutrition: Dietitian referral placed. Oral nutrition supplementation ongoing. Goal weight recovery to at minimum pre-operative weight over next 3-4 months.
- Bone health: Vitamin D supplementation increased. Consider baseline DEXA scan given post-surgical malabsorption risk and history of cancer treatment; to be ordered.
- Cancer survivorship referral placed for psychosocial support and management of long-term treatment sequelae.

RETURN TO CLINIC:
Patient will return in 2-3 weeks for pre-chemotherapy labs and final clearance to initiate adjuvant Gemzar/cape. She was given direct contact information for the nursing triage line. Family members present and engaged; discharge instructions reviewed. Questions addressed at length. Patient expressed understanding of the overall treatment plan and the significance of her pathology results.

Time spent in counseling and coordination of care: 55 minutes.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Gastrointestinal Medical Oncology
